---
title: "RP 7: Motivation for Recovery"
format: html
---
# RP 7: Motivation for Recovery

Motivation naturally rises and falls; it is not a permanent feeling. Long-term recovery relies less on waiting to "feel ready" and more on reconnecting with values and consequences regularly. Motivation becomes more stable when you keep your reasons visible and specific.

A practical strategy is to separate short-term urges from long-term goals. Urges ask, "How do I feel right now?" Recovery asks, "What life am I building?"

Motivation is better understood as a practice than a feeling. Repeated contact with values, consequences, and recovery goals helps stabilize commitment during periods of fatigue, stress, or mixed feelings.

## Values-to-Action Exercise
| What I want to protect | How alcohol harms this | One recovery action this week |
|---|---|---|
|  |  |  |
|  |  |  |
|  |  |  |

::: {.callout-tip}
### Skill rehearsal

Treat this as a rehearsal for moments when motivation is low and pressure is high.

Read your table each morning for one week.

Repetition builds confidence and makes healthy responses easier to access.
:::
